segments.s770-g49: The clause places the verbs before the subject (poetic word order). بر‌آشفت is a past/stative verb meaning “became upset/was alarmed.” و simply links the two actions. جوشن بپوشید is a compound of the noun جوشن “cuirass/armor” with the past-tense prefixed verb بپوشید “put on/wore”; شاه at the end is the subject applying to both verbs (’the king became alarmed and put on his armor’).
segments.s770-g50: This is a short predicative clause with شد (past of شدن) as the main verb ‘became.’ از گرد گیتی is a prepositional/nominal phrase: گرد here functions as ‘dust/from about’ (’from the dust of the world’ or ‘around the world’), گیتی = ‘the world,’ and سراسر سیاه is the predicate complement ‘entirely black.’ Word order is subject (implied from context) + شد + complement.
segments.s770-g51: چو is the subordinating conjunction ‘when.’ The noun phrase نیروی پرخاش ترکان is genitive/attributive: نیروی (force) modified by پرخاش (attack/aggression) and then by ترکان (of the Turks) — ‘the Turks’ assaulting force.’ بدید is the past tense ‘saw’; subject is implied from the narrative.
segments.s770-g52: Two coordinated past actions. بزد is a simple past-sense verb ‘he struck / smote.’ دست is the instrument/word ‘hand’ here (i.e., ‘struck with his hand’). و links the second action: تیغ (’sword’) is the object of برکشید, and the compound prepositional phrase از میان + برکشید means ‘drew out/from the midst’ — here idiomatically ‘drew his sword (from its sheath).’ Word order places the verb بزد before its object, a common epic inversion.
segments.s770-g53: به پیش سپاه اندر آمد is a prepositional locative + verb phrase: به پیش ('to the front / before'), سپاه ('the host/army') and اندر آمد ('came in/advanced'). چو پیل is a simile (‘like an elephant’). The verb آمد is past ‘came,’ and the simile follows it to describe manner.
segments.s770-g54: زمین شد is a simple past-state: ‘the ground became…’ به کردار is a prepositional phrase meaning ‘in the manner/likeness (of).’ دریای نیل is the simile’s target ‘the sea of the Nile’ (دریای = sea, نیل = Nile). Overall: the earth took on the appearance of the Nile-sea (a poetic simile).
segments.s770-g55: چو again ‘when.’ The phrase بر لشکر ترک بر حمله برد is an idiomatic transitive construction: بر + لشکر ترک = ‘against the Turkish army,’ بر حمله برد = ‘carried out an attack / launched an assault.’ برد here is the narrative past verb ‘carried out/launched’ (an older/poetic use). Word order places prepositional elements before the verb.
segments.s770-g56: پس is sequential ‘then/afterwards.’ پشت او در نماند is literal: پشت = ‘rear/behind,’ او = ‘his,’ در نماند = negative past of ماندن ‘did not remain/was not left inside’ (i.e., ‘no rear remained’). ایچ گرد (ایچ = ‘nothing/none,’ گرد here functioning adverbially as ‘at all/around’) strengthens the negation — together: ‘then not a thing remained at his back.’
segments.s770-g65: فراوان is an adverbial/quantifying word placed before the object phrase to mean 'many' or 'plentifully'. از آن نامداران is a partitive/genitive phrase 'of those famed ones' placed before the verb as is common in Persian poetry. بکشت is an archaic past-tense verb form (third-person singular) meaning 'he killed/struck down'; the subject is omitted and picked up from context (the shah/warrior).
segments.s770-g66: چو here introduces a temporal clause 'when'. بیچاره‌تر is a comparative adjective 'more wretched/helpless'. گشت is the past of گشتن 'became' (turned into). بنمود پشت is a light-verb + noun construction: بنمود (showed/turned) plus پشت (back) yields the idiom 'showed his back' = 'turned his back / retreated'.
segments.s770-g67: ز ترکان is a prepositional/partitive phrase 'of the Turks' or 'among the Turks'. بسی بود is an existential phrase 'there were many'. در پشت اوی is a locative phrase 'in/at his rear' (اوی is an archaic form of the third-person pronoun 'him/his'), so overall: 'many Turks were at his rear.' بود is the past/existential verb.
segments.s770-g68: یکی is an indefinite 'one/a certain'. کابلی‌تیغ is a compound name 'Kabuli blade/sword' describing the weapon. در مشت اوی is a locative phrase 'in his fist/hand' (مشت here = fist/hand); word order is noun phrase → locative, describing that one of them held such a blade.
segments.s770-g69: همی‌تاخت is an imperfective/habitual past-form with the archaic prefix همی- meaning 'kept charging / was charging'. جوشان is an adverbial participle 'seething/boiling' describing the manner. چو introduces a simile 'as/like', and از ابر برق is a poetic image 'a lightning from a cloud' (از = 'from', ابر = 'cloud', برق = 'lightning').
segments.s770-g70: یکی is again 'one/a certain' used to introduce a single striking object. آسیا here is the noun 'mill' (understood in imagery as a millstone). بد is used adverbially to mean 'harshly/violently' (poetic use of 'bad/evil' as manner), and بر آن آب زرق is the prepositional phrase 'upon that gleaming/ornamented surface' (آب زرق = glitter/finery). The whole is a condensed poetic image: 'a millstone struck upon that gleaming surface.'
segments.s770-g71: فرود آمد is a compound verb meaning 'fell' or 'came down' (past). از باره is a causal/agentive phrase 'from the onslaught/assault' (باره here = burst/assault/shower). شاه جهان is a genitive/title 'the king (ruler) of the world' specifying whose onslaught it was. The construction places the cause ('از باره شاه جهان') before the verb for poetic emphasis.
segments.s770-g72: ز marks cause/agency here ('because of' or 'from'). بدخواه means 'ill-wisher' or 'enemy'. در آسیا شد نهان is a result clause: در آسیا 'in the mill' (آسیا = mill) and شد نهان 'became hidden' (شد as auxiliary/copula + نهان 'hidden'). Syntactically it states that, on account of the ill-wisher, someone became hidden in the mill.
segments.s770-g41: This is a nominal subject phrase + predication in past tense. جهاندار is the subject 'the sovereign'; زین خود is an emphatic/genitive-like phrase ('his own' / 'himself') with خود emphasizing possession or identity; نه آگاه بود is the predicative clause where نه (classical/poetic negation) precedes the adjective آگاه and بود is the past copula 'was' (so: 'was not aware'). Word order is subject + modifier + predicate, and the negation attaches to the predicate, not to the subject.
segments.s770-g42: که introduces a subordinate clause 'that...'. ماهوی سوریش functions as the clause's subject (here treated as a proper/name-like phrase), with the pronominal suffix -ش on سور (سورِ + ش) marking a relation/possession or forming the name phrase; بدخواه بود is the predicate: بدخواه 'ill-wisher/hostile' as an adjective/noun and بود the past copula 'was'. Overall: 'that X was hostile.'
segments.s770-g43: به is the preposition introducing a temporal phrase ('at'). شبگیر گاه is a compound nominal: شبگیر acts adjectivally 'of the night-watch/nocturnal' modifying گاه 'time/hour'. سپیده‌دمان is an adverbial/temporal noun meaning 'daybreak' and reinforces the hour. The whole phrase gives the time frame: 'at the night-watch hour, at dawn.'
segments.s770-g44: سواری is the subject 'a rider/horseman'. سوی خسرو is a prepositional phrase 'toward Khosrow' (سوی + proper name). آمد is the verb 'came' and is followed by دوان, an adverb/verb-form indicating manner ('running/galloping') so آمد دوان = 'came running/rode in haste' — a common poetic sequence where two motion words stack to show manner.
segments.s770-g45: که introduces reported speech/subordination: ماهوی is the reporter (name-like), گوید is the verb 'says/said' used here in the narrative present (a frequent epic device) meaning 'he says/said', and the second که begins the embedded clause 'that an army came' with آمد سپاه 'an army came' (آمد functioning as past/occurrence verb and سپاه the subject). The structure is: 'Māhūy says that (the) army came.'
segments.s770-g46: ز ترکان is a prepositional/poetic phrase 'from/about the Turks'; کنون is the temporal adverb 'now'; بر here is the preposition 'against/on' and introduces the target of the query; چه رایست is an inverted question-like phrase meaning 'what is the counsel/decision' (رایست is an archaic/poetic nominal/predicate related to 'rāy' = opinion/decision), and شاه ('the king') appears as the possessor/agent 'the king's decision'. The word order is inverted for a rhetorical question: literally 'from the Turks now against what is the king's counsel?'
segments.s770-g47: This is a nominal/or appositive listing of high titles. سپهدار is the title 'spahbed/commander'; خانست is a predicative adjectival/nominal meaning 'is khan-like / is a khan' (خان + -ست, an archaic copula/adjectival form). و connects the second title: فغفور چین 'the faghfur (imperial title) of China' — چین is a genitive/complement identifying which faghfur. The line lists ranks/titles in parallel.
segments.s770-g48: سپاهش is سپاه + enclitic -ش 'his army'; همی is an adverb 'continually/still' (classical presentational particle); برنتابد is an archaic/poetic verb-form (here lexicalized) meaning roughly 'makes (the earth) unable to bear' or 'trembles/overwhelms' (the negative-like element is internal to the older form); زمین is the affected object 'the earth/ground'. The clause therefore says 'his army continually makes the earth not endure' — i.e. 'his army shakes the earth.'
segments.s770-g161: هر آنکس که … ندید is a relative clause: هر آنکس 'whoever' (literally 'each that person') + که 'who/that' introduces the clause. او فر یزدان is a genitival phrase where فر 'far' (royal/divine glory) is possessed by او 'him' and modified by یزدان 'divine/God' (i.e. 'his divine glory'). ندید is the past tense negative of دیدن (here 'did not see' / 'has not perceived'), completing the relative clause: 'whoever did not see his divine glory.'
segments.s770-g162: ازین آسیابان بباید شنید is the main clause. ازین = از + این 'from this' (poetic fused form), آسیابان 'the miller' (agent/source) and بباید (impersonal modal) 'one ought/must' + شنید (infinitival/root form of شنیدن) functions as the verbal complement meaning 'hear of/learn about'. Together: 'one must hear/learn it from this miller.'
segments.s770-g163: پر از گوهر نابسود افسرش: پر از 'full of' (پر followed by the preposition از meaning 'of' here) governs گوهر 'gems/jewels' which is further qualified by the adjective نابسود 'unfaded/undiminished' that follows the noun (typical Persian order: noun + modifier). افسرش is افسر 'helmet/crest' with the enclitic possessive -ش 'his', so literally 'his helmet was full of unfading gems.'
segments.s770-g164: ز دیبای چینی فروزان برش: ز (poetic variant of از) introduces the material/ornament 'from/of'; دیبای چینی 'Chinese brocade/silk' is a noun phrase where چینی specifies the provenance/type of دیبا; فروزان is a predicative/attributive adjective 'radiant, gleaming'. برش here is the enclitic construction بر + -ش (on/over + him/it) indicating 'on it/him' — so the whole phrase describes a radiant Chinese brocade shining upon it/him.
segments.s770-g165: بهاریست گویی در اردیبهشت: بهاریست is a predicative adjective-verb 'is springlike / it is spring'; گویی here is parenthetical 'as one might say / it seems' (a modal/hedging particle), and در اردیبهشت is a locative phrase 'in Ordibehesht' specifying the comparison's season/month. Word order is copular/adjectival with an adverbial parenthetical.
segments.s770-g166: به بالای او سرو دهقان نکشت: به بالای او 'to/above his height' (به + بالای + او gives the locus 'above him'); سرو دهقان is an object-like phrase 'a peasant's cypress' or 'a cypress (of the) peasant' placed before the verb for emphasis, and نکشت is a negative past verb meaning 'did not plant' (ن- + کشت/کاشت) so the clause means 'no peasant planted a cypress above him' — literal SOV order with topicalized object.
segments.s770-g121: به سه روز is a temporal phrase meaning 'for three days' (به + quantity indicating duration). شاه جهان را is a definite noun phrase 'the king of the world' marked by را (the object/ topical marker) — here it presents the person affected by what follows. ز + رزم is an adverbial/relational phrase 'with regard to/from battle' (classical poetic ز = modern از), so the whole segment sets up that, for three days, with respect to fighting the king was (in that state). Word order (time → topic → adverbial) is typical of Classical Persian.
segments.s770-g122: نبود is the past negative copula meaning 'there was not' (third person past). ایچ is a classical emphatic/indefinite meaning 'any/anything' or 'at all' (equivalent to modern هیچ); here it negates existence of something. پردازش is a deverbal noun meaning 'arrangement/preparation' (here 'preparing/setting out'), governing the following coordinated nouns خوان و بزم ('table/banquet and revelry'). Together the clause says 'there was no preparation of table and feast.'
segments.s770-g123: بدو گفت شاه reads literally 'to him said the king' — classical Persian often places the indirect object/goal (بدو = 'to him') before گفت (said), then the subject شاه follows; this order emphasizes the recipient. آنچ داری is a relative-like phrase 'that which you have' (آنچ = 'that which', داری = present/possessive 'you have' from داشتن). بیار is the imperative 'bring' addressing the addressee; so the clause is 'the king said to him: bring what you have.'
segments.s770-g124: خورش نیز با برسم آید به کار: خورش نیز 'his/that food also' (نیز = 'also'), با here functions as 'if/when' or 'by/with' (classical connective), برسم آید is a fixed verbal phrase meaning 'it will come to hand / be of use' (برسم from رسیدن in sense 'to be sufficient / reach'); به کار 'to use' or 'in (the) work'—together: 'his food too, if it comes, will be of use.' Verb آید is present/future potential used narratively.
segments.s770-g125: سبک مرد بی‌مایه: سبک (here 'frivolous' or 'light of character') modifies مرد ('man'); بی‌مایه is a compound adjective 'without substance/poor'—so 'a frivolous, impoverished man'. چبین نهاد: نهاد is past of نهادن 'placed' (or 'set down'); چبین is a proper name (Chubin) here, so the verb takes the name as its object: 'he placed/set down Chubin.' Word order adjective → noun → adjective → verb (+ object) is normal.
segments.s770-g126: برو is an interjection/imperative 'go' or here a colloquial 'then' introducing the action that follows; تره و نان کشکین نهاد: تره و نان ('greens and bread') is the coordinated direct object; کشکین is an adjectival/qualifying word 'made with kashk' or 'lean' modifying نان; نهاد (again) is past 'placed/put down' — so 'then he placed greens and bread of kashk (or lean bread).' The sequence lists items placed for the meal.
segments.s770-g127: ببرسم شتابید و آمد به راه: ببرسم is a subjunctive/optative-inflected form (be- + رسَم) meaning 'that he may reach' or 'to reach' (here expressing purpose/result); شتابید is past 'he hastened'; و آمد 'and he came'; به راه 'onto the road' — literal sequence 'so that he might reach, he hurried and came onto the road.' Note the classical use of ب- prefixed to a verb stem to form purpose or potential.
segments.s770-g128: به جایی که بود اندران واژگاه: به جایی 'to a place' (به = to/toward + جایی = place); که بود اندران is a relative clause 'which was therein' (که = which; بود = was; اندران = 'in it/among them'); واژگاه is a place-name or noun literally 'word-place' (here likely a fixed location). So the whole modifier specifies 'to the place which was in that waẓgāh.' Word order places the relative clause after the head noun, as in Persian.
segments.s770-g57: همه = "everyone/they all" (subject). پشت بر تاجور گاشتند is a poetic/archaic expression: literally “they put their backs upon Tajur,” i.e. they turned their backs on or abandoned Tajur. گاشتند is an older past plural form (related to گذاشتند) meaning “they placed/put/caused” and here conveys the act of turning away; the verb comes at the end, as is normal in Persian.
segments.s770-g58: میان سوارانش بگذاشتند: میان = “among/amongst”; سوارانش = “his horsemen/riders” (with the possessive -ش); بگذاشتند (بـ+گذاشتند) is the past plural “they placed/left” (here best read as “they left (him)”). The object (him/Tajur) is not repeated and is understood from the previous clause, so the verb simply places that understood object among his horsemen.
segments.s770-g59: چو = “when.” برگشت is the past form “returned.” In older/poetic Persian the verb can appear before the full subject phrase, so برگشت ماهوی شاه جهان = “when Māhūy, king of the world, returned.” ماهوی here functions as the (proper) name or epithet and شاه جهان is an appositive title.
segments.s770-g60: بدانست = “he knew it / perceived it” (past tense, 3rd-person singular). نیرنگ او = “his stratagem/trick” (نیرنگ + the 3rd-person pronoun او); در نهان = “in secret / hidden.” Word order is verb + object + locative phrase, and the pronoun او is a resumptive 3rd‑person reference whose antecedent is from the surrounding lines.
segments.s770-g61: چنین بود = “such/thus was.” ماهوی را رای و راه uses the classical Persian construction where را marks the possessor (ماهوی را = “of Māhūy”), so the phrase reads “such was Māhūy’s رای (counsel/plan) and راه (way).” Note the order: demonstrative/adverbial (چنین) + copula بود + possessor marked by را + possessed nouns.
segments.s770-g62: که = “that” (introducing the result/content clause). او ماند = “he remained” (ماند, past of ماندن). اندر میان سپاه = “within/in the midst of the army” (اندر is an archaic/poetic preposition = in/among). The clause states the consequence or fact of the preceding description.
segments.s770-g63: شهنشاه = “the king of kings / emperor” (subject). در جنگ = “in battle” (locative phrase). شد ناشکیب = “became impatient” (شد as copular/auxiliary meaning “became” + adjective ناشکیب “impatient/restless”). The construction expresses a change of state in the past.
segments.s770-g64: همی‌زد is the imperfect/continuous poetic form (همی + زد) meaning “he kept striking/was striking.” The verb is followed by a series of prepositional/oblque phrases introduced by به used instrumentally/directionally: به تیغ = “with/by (the) sword,” به پای = “with/by (his) foot(s)” (i.e. kicking or striking with the feet), و رکیب = “and (at) rivals/opponents.” The verb-first order with repeated prepositional phrases is typical in older poetry.
segments.s770-g1: The clause uses the indefinite existential pattern یکی + noun + بود: یکی پهلوان بود = 'there was a certain hero/champion.' گسترده‌کام is a post‑verbal adjective describing disposition ('broad‑willed' or 'ambitious'). بود is the past copula 'was' and agrees with the preceding nominal phrase.
segments.s770-g2: نژادش is the noun 'his lineage/stock' with the third‑person possessive clitic -ش. The preposition ز (poetic 'from') introduces the ancestor/place: ز طرخان = 'from Tarkhān.' The coordinating و links the next phrase, and بنام here means 'called/named' (short for به نام). The overall structure states the person's descent and his name.
segments.s770-g3: نشستش is 'his seat/settlement' derived from نشستن with the possessive clitic -ش (literally 'his sitting/seat'). به شهر سمرقند بود is a locative clause: به + place + بود ('his seat was at/in the city of Samarkand'). The verb بود is the past copula.
segments.s770-g4: بران is a poetic locative/adverbial 'on/thereupon' referring back to that place; مرز means 'border/frontier.' چندیش is an indefinite/approximate quantifier here ('several / some'), modifying پیوند ('ties, bonds, alliances'). بود is again the past copula, so the clause says that on that border he had several bonds.
segments.s770-g5: چو is the temporal subordinating conjunction 'when.' ماهوی (here treated as a descriptive name/epithet) + بدبخت 'the ill‑fated/ill‑starred' form the subject phrase; خودکامه شد uses شد 'became' plus the adjective 'خودکامه' ('self‑willed, autocratic'), so the whole clause means 'when X became autocratic.' شد is the verbal predicate 'became.'
segments.s770-g6: The clause is passive/impersonal in shape: ازو 'from him' + نزد بیژن 'to/at Bijan (in Bijan's presence)' + یکی نامه شد literally 'one letter happened' — i.e. 'a letter came from him to Bijan.' شد here functions as 'happened/arrived.' The word order (source — recipient — item — شد) is typical of classical Persian reporting.
segments.s770-g7: که‌ای here functions as an archaic/vocative address ('O' or 'O you') followed by the appellation پهلوان زاده 'born of a champion/offspring of a hero.' بی‌گزند is an adjective 'unharmed / unmarred' describing that lineage. The phrase addresses the addressee with a laudatory epithet.
segments.s770-g8: یکی رزم is 'a certain battle' (indefinite subject). پیش آمدت is a two‑word verb phrase: پیش 'before/forward' + آمدت (آمد + enclitic ‑ت 'to you') — literally 'came before you' or idiomatically 'befell/has arisen for you.' سودمند is a predicate adjective 'useful/profitable.' Word order places the indefinite subject first and the descriptive predicate at the end, a common epic structure.
segments.s770-g9: که is the subordinating conjunction 'that' introducing this clause. شاه جهان is a noun phrase 'the king of the world' (subject). با سپاه is a prepositional phrase 'with an army' marking accompaniment and follows the subject (typical Persian order: subject + adjunct). ایدرست is an archaic/poetic predicative form built from درست ('right/upright') with a vocative/affirmative flavor; here it functions as the clause's predicate meaning roughly 'is righteous/true' or 'is indeed so'.
segments.s770-g10: This string contains three nominal predicates linked by conjunctions. ابا appears as an archaic particle/verb form (listed lexically as 'to refuse') but in this poetic context it functions to introduce the state that follows rather than its literal lexical sense. تاج و گاهست is a compound where تاج ('crown') + و + گاه ('seat/throne' in context) + -ست (poetic/concise = است 'is') yields 'is crown and throne' (i.e., 'is crowned and seated'); the -ست ending is the archaic/poetic copula. و با افسرست parallels that structure: با + افسر ('helmet') + -ست = 'is with a helmet' (or 'is a commander/helmeted one' — افسر is a poetic word that can mean 'helmet' or 'officer').
segments.s770-g11: گر is the conditional 'if'. آیی is the (present/subjunctive-like) form of 'come/arrive' meaning 'you come into/obtain' here. The noun series سر و تاج و گاهش lists possessions — 'head (chief), crown and گاهِش (his seat/throne)' — with تو (you) attached to گاهش to mark possession/agreement, and راست is a predicate/adjectival copula meaning 'are rightful/justly yours'. Word order: conditional + verb + objects + possessor + predicative adjective.
segments.s770-g12: همان is a demonstrative 'that same' (emphatic). گنج و چتر lists two nouns 'treasure and canopy/umbrella' (چتر here = canopy/cover, figuratively a royal canopy). سیاهش is a genitive/attributive form 'its/that black' (black/dark) describing the canopy; تو راست repeats the possessive 'you' plus the predicate 'are right/are yours'. The clause thus asserts that the mentioned treasure and its dark canopy belong rightly to you.
segments.s770-g13: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. بیژن is the personal subject 'Bijan'. نگه کرد is a light-verb construction: نگه 'a look/gaze' + کرد ('did') = 'he looked' (common Persian pattern: noun + کردن). و آن نامه دید continues with و + آن نامه + دید = 'and he saw that letter' (دید is the past tense 'saw').
segments.s770-g14: This clause repeats the perception verb: جهان is a proper name or noun 'Jahan/the world' acting as the thing seen; پیش functions as a preposition/adverb 'before/in front of' or 'in the presence of'; ماهوی here (in this poetic context) is an epithet (lexically 'essential' or a name-form) and خودکامه is an adjective 'self-willed/autocratic' modifying that person; دید closes the clause as the seeing verb 'he saw'. Word order: object (جهان) + local/adverbial modifier + adjective phrase + verb.
segments.s770-g15: به دستور is an adverbial phrase 'by (the) order' or 'on command' (به + noun). گفت is the past-tense verb 'he said'. ای + سر راستان is the vocative: ای 'O' + سر 'head/chief' used as a title + راستان 'the righteous/upright' (an epithet). Together, ای سر راستان is a direct address 'O upright chief' or 'O righteous leader'.
segments.s770-g16: چه is the interrogative 'what'. داری is the second-person singular present of داشتن 'to have', but in the idiom چه داری بیاد literally 'what do you have in memory' = 'what do you remember'. بیاد ('in memory') is an adverbial phrase; اندرین داستان 'in this story' (اندرین = in this, داستان = tale/story) is the locative phrase specifying where/what the memory pertains to. The clause is a direct question: 'What do you recall about this tale?'
segments.s770-g153: چنین (thus) is a sentence adverb that frames the manner of the reply. داد is the past tense verb 'gave' serving here as the main verb. پاسخ is the direct object 'answer/reply'; ورا is the enclitic/old pronominal prepositional phrase 'to him' (object of دادن). ترسکار is an agent noun 'the fearful/timid one' and functions as the subject performing 'gave the answer'. Word order is poetic (manner-adverb + verb + object + indirect object + subject).
segments.s770-g154: که introduces reported speech or content clause 'that …'. من is the first-person subject 'I'. بار here is 'burden/load' used transitively with کردن. کردم is the 1st-person past of کردن 'I did/made/bore' (poetic past form). همی is a temporal/emphatic adverb (here 'still/indeed') modifying the following predicate. خواستار is a predicative noun/adjective 'a petitioner/desirous one' describing the speaker's state. Together the clause reports what the fearful man said.
segments.s770-g155: در here is the noun 'door' (archaic/neuter) and آسیا is 'mill'; together 'در آسیا' = 'the mill's door'. را is the definite/object marker attaching to the phrase. گشادم is an archaic/poetic past form meaning 'I opened' (cf. گشودم). به خشم is a prepositional phrase 'in/with anger' modifying the verb. The segment literally: 'I opened the mill-door in anger.'
segments.s770-g156: چنان دان is an exhortative/imperative-styled clause 'consider/know it so' — دان here is 'to consider/know' in an older register. که begins the content clause reporting what is to be considered. خورشید دیدم به چشم is a simple past clause 'I saw the sun with (my) eye(s)', where دیدم is 1st-person past 'I saw' and به چشم is an instrument/means 'with the eye' (i.e., with my own eyes). The whole segment links as 'so know that I saw the sun with my eyes.'
segments.s770-g157: دو نرگس is a poetic epithet for 'two eyes' (نرگس used figuratively). چو is a simile marker 'like/as'. نر آهو 'male gazelle' names the comparison image; اندر هراس 'in fear/terror' completes the simile ('like a male gazelle in alarm'). The phrase describes the terrified, gazelle-like quality of the pair of eyes.
segments.s770-g158: دیده 'eyes' (noun) begins the second hemistich. چو introduces another simile 'as if/like'. از شب گذشته is a perfective/adverbial phrase literally 'having passed (through) the night' — گذشته is past/‘gone’ used as participle. سه پاس uses پاس in the sense 'a watch/guard' (i.e., a threefold watch of the night). Together: 'two eyes as if they had gone through three watches of the night' (implying tired, sleepless eyes).
segments.s770-g159: چو is a temporal conjunction here 'when'. خورشید is the subject 'the sun'. گشتست is an older/periphrastic past 'had become/was turned' (i.e., had passed/changed position). زو is the poetic preposition 'from it' (referring back to the mill), and the following و is the coordinating conjunction 'and' linking to آسیا 'the mill' (the sequence is compact/poetic). The clause sets the temporal scene 'When the sun had passed (relative to) the mill…'.
segments.s770-g160: خورش here means 'a cooked dish/food' (not the sun). نان خشک 'dry bread' is a simple nominal phrase describing the meagre food. و is 'and'. نشستش is the past tense 'he sat down' with the enclitic -ش marking 'him/it' (so 'he sat himself down'). گیا (گیا) is the noun 'grass, vegetation' and serves as the locative object 'on the grass'. The segment says: 'his food was dry bread, and he sat on the grass.'
segments.s770-g105: نگه کرد is a light-verb construction: نگه (a noun 'gaze') + کرد ('did') = 'gazed'. خسرو is the subject; بدو is the prepositional pronoun 'to him/at him'. خیره ماند is a predicate: خیره ('staring/astonished') + ماند ('remained' in past), i.e. 'remained astonished'. Note the poetic word order (verb phrase before subject).
segments.s770-g106: بدان here functions adverbially as 'with that/thereupon' (classical 'به + آن' compressed), introducing the state named by خیرگی ('astonishment, stupefaction'). نام یزدان بخواند = 'he called/uttered the name of Yezdān (God)', where بخواند is a past/preterite third-person form meaning 'he uttered/called' (i.e. invoked).
segments.s770-g107: بدو گفت = 'he said to him' (بدو = 'to him'; گفت = 'said' in past). کای is the vocative particle 'O'; شاه is the addressee's title; خورشید‌روی is a compound adjective 'sun-faced' (praising his appearance) used in direct address.
segments.s770-g108: برین آسیا = 'to/at this mill' (برین = بر + این, 'on/at this'); چون رسیدی تو = temporal clause 'when you arrive' (چون functioning like 'when/as'); تو is emphatic 'you'. گوی is an imperative/hortative 'say!' ( classical poetic form of گفتن), telling him to report when he reaches the mill.
segments.s770-g109: چه introduces an exclamatory question 'what/how' (rhetorical). جای = 'place'. نشستت is نشست + ـت (your sitting/seat) — 'your seat'. بود is the past copula 'was' (used here in a rhetorical clause), and آسیا is the noun 'mill' (subject/correlate). The order is poetic/inverted: 'What place was your seat? — the mill...'.
segments.s770-g110: پر از = 'full of' (پر here = 'full'); از = 'of'; گندم = 'wheat'; و = 'and'; خاک = 'earth/dust'; و = 'and'; چندی گیا = 'some plants/foliage' (چندی = 'some/several', گیا = 'plants/vegetation'). Together: 'full of wheat and dust and some plants.'
segments.s770-g111: چه مردی is an exclamation 'what a man' (celebratory). بدین here is classical 'ب + این' = 'with this/possessing this' (introducing qualities). فر = 'farr' (royal/divine glory), و این برز = 'and this height/exaltedness' (برز = elevation/exaltation), و چهر = 'and visage/face' — listing the noble attributes the man possesses.
segments.s770-g112: که introduces the relative/result clause. چون تو = 'one like you' (چون = 'like/as'); نبیند is negative present/poetic perfect of دیدن with the negative prefix ن- meaning 'does not/has not seen' (here: 'has never seen'); همانا سپهر = emphatic 'truly the sky/heavens' (subject of 'has not seen'). Overall: 'that the heavens truly have never seen anyone like you.'
segments.s770-g17: به یاری is a prepositional phrase meaning “with the help of.” ماهوی‌گر is a conditional/poetic sequence here: ماهوی (a name or epithet) + -گر (the poetic conditional/’if’ particle), so the whole phrase functions as the protasis “if by Mahuy’s aid.” The subject and object that follow (من سپاه) are placed after this conditional phrase for emphasis.
segments.s770-g18: برانم is a first-person present/subjunctive-like form “I lead/drive (on)” with the -م personal ending. شود is a third-person auxiliary/verb meaning “becomes/will be” and forms the apodosis (result clause). کارم is کار + ـم (possesive ‘my’); in classical poetry the possessive -م may appear separated in editions. ایدر (اینجا) is an archaic locative “here.” تباه is a predicative adjective “ruined.” Word order: verb (برانم) appears before the clause 'شود کارم ایدر تباه' giving the conditional result “(if I lead ...) then my work here will become ruined.”
segments.s770-g19: به من here marks the person affected (“me”). برکند is a past/perfective (or classical present with past sense) verb “uprooted / dislodged / tore away” and precedes its subject (شاه چینی) — a common poetic inversion. فسوس (‘insidious whisper/insinuation’) names the manner or instrument: “by his whispering.”
segments.s770-g20: مرا is the object “me” (here printed as the clitic m plus the object marker را split in the line). بی‌منش is an accusative predicative adjective “without manners / base.” خواند is a past-tense verb “he called/named” (not ‘read’); it takes مرا as object. و چاپلوس adds a second predicate “and (called me) a flatterer.” The sequence is therefore “he called me base and a flatterer.”
segments.s770-g21: وگرنه is a conditional/concessive “otherwise.” کنَم is first-person present/subjunctive or modal “I would do / I might do.” گوید here is a reportative verb meaning “(he) says” introducing reported speech; in the supplied senses list this verbal sense is not present, so it is treated as non-matching. از + بیم = “out of fear / because of fear,” and کرد is the past-tense verb “he did/acted.” The clause assembles as a reported remark: “Otherwise I would do it — he says he acted out of fear.”
segments.s770-g22: همی‑ترسد uses the archaic prefix همی- to mark continuous or habitual action plus ترسد (3rd person) “he fears/keeps fearing.” از روز ننگ و نبرد is a prepositional phrase “of (the) day of shame and battle,” with روز (day) governing the genitive-style phrase ننگ و نبرد. Overall: “he continually fears the day of shame and battle.”
segments.s770-g23: چنین داد is a concise verb phrase “thus gave” (i.e. “thus he gave/issued”). دستور (order/command) is the direct object and پاسخ (reply/answer) specifies what was ordered. بدوی is an archaic/adjectival/adverbial form meaning roughly “immediate/rough/primitive” or “in reply”; here it qualifies the kind of reply ordered. Classical Persian frequently places the verb before its objects for emphasis.
segments.s770-g24: که‌ای here introduces direct speech or the content of the command; that + vocative construction (که ای → “that is: O …”). شیر‌دل is a compounded adjective “lion‑hearted,” مرد is the noun “man,” and پرخاشجوی is an adjectival epithet “aggressive / quarrelsome.” Together they form the direct address: “O lion‑hearted, quarrelsome man.”
segments.s770-g97: خور خویش زان آسیا ساختی — Subject + possessive + source + verb: خور here is a poetic noun meaning 'sun' (metaphorical), followed by خویش (خوی + ش) that marks possession ('his/your own'). زان is the archaic/poetic preposition 'from that', and ساختی is 2nd-person singular past ('you made/constructed'). Word order is topical: direct object (خور خویش) precedes the source phrase (زان آسیا) and the verb.
segments.s770-g98: به کاری جزین خود نپرداختی — به کاری introduces the indirect object or sphere ('to/with any work'); کاری here is intended as 'work/occupation' (not fully captured in the provided sense list). جزین means 'except this/other than this' and comes before the verb for emphasis. خود is reflexive/emphatic ('yourself'), and نپرداختی is the negative past of پرداختن ('you did not attend to/engage in'). The whole clause says the subject did nothing but the mentioned activity.
segments.s770-g99: گوی دید بر سان سرو بلند — دید is the past-tense verb 'he saw/beheld'. گوی here is ambiguous/poetic; it can mean 'sphere/round form' or function idiomatically (appearance), but in this line it most naturally reads as a concrete 'rounded form/ball' that is being perceived. بر سان = 'in the manner of / like' (بر + سان), followed by سرو بلند 'tall cypress' as the simile. The phrase orders the visible object first, then its likening.
segments.s770-g100: نشسته بران سنگ چون مستمند — نشسته is a past participle/adjectival predicate 'sitting'. بران is an archaic contraction of بر آن ('on that'), followed by سنگ 'stone/rock'. چون + مستمند makes the simile 'like a mendicant/beggar'. The participle describes the posture/location of the previously seen object.
segments.s770-g101: یکی افسری خسروی بر سرش — یکی introduces an indefinite 'a certain/one', افسری (here) = 'diadem/crown', خسروی is an adjective 'royal'. بر سرش is a prepositional phrase 'upon his head' (بر + سر + -ش possessive). Word order places the indefinite + noun + modifier before the locative phrase.
segments.s770-g102: درفشان ز دیبای چینی برش — درفشان is an adjective/participle 'shining, glittering'. ز is the poetic genitive/preposition 'of/from', so ز دیبای چینی = 'of Chinese brocade/silk'. برش here is the enclitic construction equivalent to بر + ش ('on him' / 'upon him') in compact form; the clause modifies the diadem as glittering with that fabric. Note the archaic use of ز and the enclitic pronoun.
segments.s770-g103: به پیکر یکی کفش زرین بپای — به پیکر is a locative phrase 'on his body/person'. یکی کفش زرین 'a (single/certain) golden shoe' is the item; بپای is an archaic contraction of به پای ('on the foot' / 'at the foot'), so the phrase means 'a golden shoe on his foot'. The verb is omitted (a stylistic nominal clause), so the nouns and postpositional phrase carry the predication.
segments.s770-g104: ز خوشاب و زر آستین قبای — ز here is the poetic 'of/from' marking material. خوشاب is a technical or poetic textile name (lit. 'pleasant water') used here as a fabric or fine material; و زر = 'and gold'; آستین قبای = 'the sleeve of the robe'. The whole phrase states the sleeve's materials ('of khoshâb and gold').
segments.s770-g113: This phrase places a prepositional phrase and a subject before the verb in poetic order. از ایرانیانم = 'from/among my Iranians' (literally 'of my Iranians') and بدو = 'to him'; شاه is the subject 'the king'. In Persian poetry the verb often follows subject and complements, so this chunk functions as 'the king said to him' with an initial modifier 'from/among my Iranians'.
segments.s770-g114: هزیمت گرفتم ز توران‌سپاه is a reported first‑person statement inside that speech: هزیمت (a rout/defeat) + گرفتم (1st person past 'I took/inflicted') plus ز توران‌سپاه ('of/from the Turanian host'). The verb گرفتم is simple past first person; ز here is the poetic genitive/prepositional link 'of/from' connecting the object to توران‌سپاه.
segments.s770-g115: بدو (to him) is the indirect object; آسیابان is the speaker 'the miller' (here singular/agent). به تشویر is an adverbial phrase 'with ornament/with a flourish' (تشویر = ornamentation); گفت is the past verb 'said' (verb at end as normal). The order places the indirect object and adverbial before subject+verb, a common poetic arrangement.
segments.s770-g116: که introduces the reported content clause 'that / (i.e.) ...'. جز تنگ‌دستی = 'except poverty' (جز = except; تنگ‌دستی here = poverty/penury). مرا نیست جفت is an existential/construction meaning 'to me there is no pair/match' (مرا = 'to me/for me' with را split in the word list, نیست = 'is not/does not exist', جفت = 'pair/match'). Word order places the dative/experiencer before the copula in poetic style.
segments.s770-g117: اگر introduces a conditional 'if'. نان کشکینت = 'your meagre/lean bread' (نان = bread; کشکین with possessive -ت 'your' and here adjectival 'lean, meagre'). آید به کار is an idiom 'comes to use / is of use' where آید is present/subjunctive 'comes' and به کار = 'to/for use'.
segments.s770-g118: This short clause gives the speaker's dismissal of the previous insult. ورین = 'that/aforementioned' or 'the foregoing' (poetic demonstrative/former), ناسزا = 'insult/abuse', and تره جویبار is a metaphorical image 'stream‑herbage' or 'a scrap of greens from the stream' (تره = herb/green, جویبار = stream), meaning something of trivial value. Word order is noun phrase (modifier + noun) without an explicit verb, typical in compressed poetic lines.
segments.s770-g119: بیارم here is a 1st‑person verbal form meaning 'I will bring / I will add' (in older/poetic register the verb-form expresses a near‑future or hortatory 'I will say/add'). جزین نیز چیزی = 'besides this also something' (جزین = 'apart from this', نیز = 'also', چیزی = 'something') and که هست 'that is' (که introduces the clause; هست = 'is/exists'). The whole phrase announces an additional remark.
segments.s770-g120: خروشان بود مردم تنگ‌دست is a predicative clause: خروشان = 'roaring/tumultuous' (adjective/adverbial), بود = past copula 'were', مردم = 'people', تنگ‌دست = 'poor' (modifier). Word order places the predicate and copula before or around the subject in a compact poetic form: 'the poor people were tumultuous.'
segments.s770-g89: خروشی برآید is a short narrative clause: خروشی (a cry/clamor) is the noun subject and برآید is a third‑person singular verb (here a narrative/presentive 'arose' or 'went up'). که introduces the following subordinate / reported speech. بربند رخت is an imperative clause (بربند = 'bind/pack/wrap up', second person singular imperative) with its object رخت ('baggage/clothes') following the verb; older Persian allows the verb to precede the object.
segments.s770-g90: نبینی is the negative present/subjunctive/optative form 'you will not see' or 'you see not' used in reported speech. به جز is the fixed prepositional phrase meaning 'except' (به + جز). دخمه گور تخت is a stacked nominal phrase (doubly descriptive nouns) — دخمه ('sepulchre') qualifying گور تخت ('grave‑bed/platform'); classical Persian often strings nouns without extra particles.
segments.s770-g91: This is a descriptive nominal phrase made of two coordinated descriptions. دهان ناچریده places the adjective/participle ناچریده ('untouched/not plucked') after the noun (postpositive modifier): 'a mouth not touched.' دو دیده پرآب is a counted noun + adjective phrase ('two eyes full of water' i.e. tearful eyes); adjectives follow the noun in this register.
segments.s770-g92: همی‌بود is an imperfective or durative past ('continued to be' / 'remained so') used to state that the described condition persisted. تا is the temporal conjunction 'until' and introduces the clause برکشید آفتاب: برکشید is a third‑person singular verb here meaning 'lifted/rose' (the action performed by آفتاب, 'the sun'); note verb‑subject order (verb before noun) is common in classical Persian.
segments.s770-g93: گشاد here functions as a perfective/preterite or resultative meaning 'opened wide' / 'gaped open' and is placed before its agent (آسیابان, 'the miller'), a word order that occurs in older/fixed phrases. در آسیا is a locative prepositional phrase 'in the mill' (در + آسیا). Depending on context گشاد can be read adjectivally, but here it is best taken as the verbal action of opening the mill.
segments.s770-g94: به پشت اندرون is a layered locative expression: به (to/at) + پشت (back/rear) + اندرون (inside) = 'at the inner back / in the back interior.' بار و لختی گیا is a coordinated noun phrase 'a load and a little vegetation' (بار = 'load', و = 'and', لختی here functions as a small quantity/portion before گیا 'plants'); classical Persian often links nouns with و without additional particles.
segments.s770-g95: فرومایه‌ای is an indefinite predicate 'a lowly/wretched sort of person' (the -ای gives an indefinite sense). بود is the past copula 'was.' خسرو follows the verb (verb‑subject order) and here functions as the title/subject 'Khosrow' or 'the king.' به نام is the prepositional phrase 'in name' (به + نام) — together: 'Khosrow was lowly in name' (i.e., only nominally).
segments.s770-g96: This is a coordinated negative list introduced by repeated نه ... و نه ... (neither ... nor ...) — a common way to enumerate what someone lacks. Each noun is bare and understood as what he does not possess: تخت 'throne', گنج 'treasure', تاج 'crown', کام 'desire/fulfilment'.
segments.s770-g73: سواران is the plural subject 'the riders'. بجستن is the infinitive 'to seek' and combines with نهادند (past 'they set/placed') to form the periphrasis 'نهادند + infinitive' meaning 'they set about / began to (do)'. روی here is the noun 'face' used in the idiom روی نهادن (‘to turn one's face toward / to head for’), so the whole chunk means 'the riders turned their faces to seek (him)' — i.e. 'the riders set out to seek'.
segments.s770-g74: همه زرق ازو is a noun phrase 'all the finery of him / his entire finery' (همه = 'all', زرق = 'finery', ازو = 'of him'). شد is the past of شدن 'became', and پر از گفت‌وگوی is the predicate 'full of conversation' (پر از = 'full of', گفت‌وگوی = 'conversation / talk'). The line thus says 'all his finery became full of talk' (i.e. people talked about his glitter/ornaments).
segments.s770-g75: ازو at the front marks what is 'from him / left by him'. بازماند is the intransitive past 'remained / was left'. اسپ زرین ستام is the noun phrase 'the golden steed, (well-)praised' where زرین (golden) modifies اسپ and ستام is a participial epithet 'praised'. Together: 'From him was left the praised golden steed.'
segments.s770-g76: همان is a demonstrative 'the same / that very', and گرز و شمشیر is a coordinated noun phrase 'mace and sword'. زرین‌نیام is a compound noun 'golden scabbard' (or splendid sheath) specifying the sword. The short line lists the items left: 'the same mace and the sword with a golden scabbard.'
segments.s770-g77: بجستنش is a nominal/gerundial form built on بجستن with the enclitic -ش 'him', meaning 'the seeking of him / seeking him'. ترکان is the subject 'the Turks' and خروشان شدند is the predicate 'became roaring/tumultuous' (خروشان = 'roaring/tumultuous', شدند = 'they became'). So: 'In seeking him, the Turks became tumultuous.'
segments.s770-g78: از آن باره is a prepositional phrase 'from/for that matter / because of that event' (از = 'from', آن = 'that', باره = 'occasion/instance'). و is the conjunction 'and'. ساز جوشان شدند = 'the instruments/music became fervent' (ساز = 'musical instrument / music', جوشان here = 'ardent/fervent', شدند = 'they became'), i.e. the music/instruments burst into vigorous sound.
segments.s770-g79: نهان گشته is a participial/perfect formation meaning 'having hidden (himself) / hidden', where گشته is the perfect participle 'become/been'. در خانه آسیا is the locative 'in the mill-house' (آسیا here = 'mill'). The clause states the location where he had taken refuge: 'hidden, he had gone into the mill-house.'
segments.s770-g80: نشست is the simple past 'he sat / settled'. از بر is a classical locative combination meaning 'upon / on top of' (از + بر used together), خشک modifies گیا to mean 'dry grass/vegetation', and لختی is an adverbial 'for a short while'. Put together: 'he sat upon the dry grass for a little while.'
segments.s770-g33: به marks the indirect object 'to/for Bersam' (prepositional phrase). فرمود is a past-tense verb 'he commanded/said' and together فرمود تا introduces a commanded content clause. تا + numeral (تا ده هزار) here functions like 'up to / to the number of' and positions the amount before the noun phrase that follows.
segments.s770-g34: نبرده‌سواران is a compound plural noun 'cavalry / battle-horsemen' and is the noun counted by 'ده هزار' that precedes it. خنجرگزار is a postpositive epithet ('dagger‑bearer') describing those horsemen; Persian commonly places such descriptive compounds after the noun.
segments.s770-g35: به مرو is a destination 'to/into Merv.' اندرون 'inside/inner quarters' specifies where the action took place. ساز here is the noun 'means/arrangement' (part of the idiom سازِ جنگ = 'preparations/contrivance of war'), and جنگ آورد (آورد = 'brought / brought about') is a verbal phrase meaning 'made/brought about war' or 'waged war' with the object (جنگ) placed before the verb as is common in Persian.
segments.s770-g36: مگر here introduces a conjectural/purposive sense ('perhaps / with the intent that'), linking the preceding military action to a motive. گنج ایران is a genitive phrase 'the treasure of Iran.' به + چنگ آورد is an idiom 'to bring into the grasp of' = 'to seize,' where چنگ 'claw/hand' is the object of the prepositional construction and آورد means 'bring/seize.'
segments.s770-g37: سپاه از بخارا is the noun phrase 'the army from Bukhara' (از marks origin). چو introduces a simile ('like/as'), and پران 'winged' is a descriptive adjective used metaphorically. تذرو (an archaic verb) here participates in the image of rapid, dispersing motion; together 'چو پران تذرو' paints the army as moving like winged beings.
segments.s770-g38: بیامد is the past verb 'came/arrived.' به یک هفته indicates the time span 'in one week' (به + duration = within/after that time). تا شهر مرو uses تا as 'as far as / to' and marks the destination reached: 'to the city of Merv.' The clause therefore states the time taken and the destination reached.
segments.s770-g39: شب تیره هنگام is a temporal phrase 'at the dark night-hour' where تیره (dark) qualifies شب and هنگام ('at the time') makes it an adverbial temporal expression. بانگ خروس is a possessive/genitive construction 'the cry/sound of the rooster' (بانگ = cry/sound, here the audible call).
segments.s770-g40: از آن مرز is a prepositional phrase 'from that frontier.' برخاست is the past intransitive verb 'rose / was heard to rise' (used idiomatically of sounds). آواز کوس is a noun phrase 'the sound/voice of the drum' (کوس = war‑drum), and the clause literally says 'from that border rose the sound of the drum' — i.e., the war‑drum was sounded there.
segments.s770-g81: چنین است is a predicative phrase meaning “such is” or “thus is.” رسم is the head noun “custom/way.” سرای فریب forms a genitive/construct (the house/abode of deceit) with فریب as its modifier; classical Persian poetry often omits the linking vowel (ezâfe), so the sequence reads as one nominal phrase “the custom of the house of deceit.”
segments.s770-g82: فرازش and نشیبش are nouns with the possessive enclitic -ش (“its”), referring back to the previously named place/thing. بلند is an adjective modifying فراز (high/lofty). The conjunction و links the two clauses; نشیبش نشیب is a tautological/poetic repetition emphasizing that its descent is indeed a true descent (the second نشیب is a noun used for emphasis). Word order is noun(+possessive) + adjective, typical of Persian.
segments.s770-g83: بدانگه که introduces a temporal clause “at that moment when….” بیدار is a predicative adjective “awake.” بد بخت is a compound attributive (bad + fortune) meaning “ill‑fated/unlucky,” and اوی is the third‑person clitic (here functioning as the subject reference “he” or a clitic referring back to someone previously mentioned). The clause thus reads “when the unfortunate one awoke.”
segments.s770-g84: بگردون کشیدی is a verbal phrase where کشیدی is the past tense (2nd person singular narrative form “you pulled/drew/made”) and بگردون (the revolving sphere/world/sky) functions as the element acted upon or as an adverbial; poetic texts often allow flexible ordering. فلک تخت اوی places فلک (the sky/heaven) with تخت اوی (“his throne”), where اوی is a possessive clitic attached to تخت. The whole line is best understood as a causative/result: “you made the sky (into) his throne.”
segments.s770-g85: کنون is a temporal adverb “now.” آسیابی (a form of آسیاب) is the noun “a mill” (used literally or metaphorically). بیامدش is a compound verb with the enclitic ‑ش (“to him”) meaning “it came to him” or “occurred to him.” بهر is the preposition “for/for the sake of,” marking purpose. Together: “Now a mill came to him for (that) purpose.”
segments.s770-g86: ز is the preposition “from/of/because of.” نوشش is نوش + possessive ‑ش, “his drink/delight.” فراوان فزون is a doubled adjectival intensifier meaning “abundantly, even more so.” بود is the past copula “was,” and زهر is the subject “poison.” Word order is slightly inverted for poetic effect (adjectives/adverbs before the verb), yielding “the poison was more abundant than his draught/delight.”
segments.s770-g87: چه is the interrogative “what.” بندی is the present/optative form of بستن used rhetorically (“can bind” or “will bind”), so the phrase asks rhetorically “what can bind…?” دل is the direct object “the heart.” اندر is the locative preposition “in/within,” and سرای فسوس is the place phrase “the house/abode of whispers/temptation” (فسوس = insidious whisper). The whole segment asks “what can bind the heart inside the house of whispering temptation?”
segments.s770-g88: که introduces a subordinate clause (here giving the reason/time: “since/when”). هزمان (ever/always/simultaneously) qualifies the following verb. به گوش آید is an impersonal/passive‑like construction “comes to the ear / is heard” with آید in the present/habitual sense. آواز کوس is the noun phrase “the sound/cry of the drum.” Together they mean “for the drum’s sound is always heard in the ear,” explaining why the heart cannot be bound.
segments.s770-g129: بر مهتر زرق شد بی‌گذار = literal order: preposition + noun + predicate. بر here is a comparative/prepositional particle 'over / more than' placed before مهتر to mark comparison. زرق شد is a predicative phrase: زرق (finery/ornament) used with شد (past of شدن) to mean 'became adorned / put on finery'. بی‌گذار follows as an adverbial/adjectival modifier meaning 'without restraint / unsparing'.
segments.s770-g130: که برسم کند زو یکی خواستار = a subordinate clause introduced by که ('that / so that'). برسم is the classical subjunctive/potential form of رسیدن ('to reach / arrive') used here in an indirect/purpose sense. کند is the 3rd-person auxiliary/verb 'does/makes' placed after the subjunctive to form a jussive/optative sense ('may cause / may be that'), and ز + و should be read together as the poetic preposition + pronoun 'زو' = 'from him / from there'. یکی خواستار is an indefinite noun phrase 'a certain petitioner / someone desirous' serving as the logical subject of the clause.
segments.s770-g131: بهر سو فرستاد ماهوی کس = بهر سو is a classical prepositional phrase 'toward that side' (بهر = toward, سو = side/direction). فرستاد is the 3rd‑person past 'sent'. ماهوی کس is a poetic noun phrase: ماهوی modifies کس — here 'ماهوی' has a celestial/ethereal nuance ('moon‑like' / 'heavenly') qualifying کس ('a person / someone') and functions as the object of فرستاد.
segments.s770-g132: ز گیتی همی شاه را جست و بس = ز گیتی 'throughout/from the world' marks the scope. همی here is the classical particle meaning 'just / then / immediately' (an emphatic/temporal particle). شاه را is the definite object 'the king' (را marks definiteness/object). جست is the past of جویا/جستن here 'sought / searched for'. و بس 'and enough' (بس = 'enough / sufficient') closes the clause: 'and that sufficed.'
segments.s770-g133: از آن آسیابان بپرسید مه = از آن آسیابان 'of/from that miller' (از + demonstrative + noun). بپرسید is an imperative/request form in classical style ('ask!' — 2nd person plural/formal), though the lemma listing may show a past sense; here it functions as a directive. مه is a vocative/interjection — in poetic usage it can mean 'O moon' or 'O great one' (a form of address); here it is a vocative particle calling the addressee.
segments.s770-g134: که برسم کرا خواهی ای روزبه = که introduces an indirect question or content clause ('that / asking which ...'). برسم is the subjunctive/potential of رسیدن ('to reach/arrive') used in the indirect question. کرا is an archaic interrogative pronoun meaning 'which / whom' (used here to ask 'by which way / which one'). خواهی is 2nd‑person singular present 'you want / you will', so the phrase as a whole means 'which way you want to take to reach (him)'. ای روزبه is the vocative 'O Ruzbeh' addressing the person spoken to.
segments.s770-g135: بدو گفت خسرو که در آسیا = بدو is a prepositional phrase 'to him / unto him' (به + او compressed), placed before گفت; گفت here is the past-tense verb 'said' (classical word order puts the object/indirect object before the verb). خسرو is the speaker's name. که introduces the reported content 'that'. در آسیا is the locative 'in the mill.'
segments.s770-g136: نشستست کنداوری بر گیا = نشستست is an archaic/perfective form meaning 'he sat / was sitting' (3rd person). کنداوری is a proper name (the person who sits). بر گیا is a locative phrase 'upon the vegetation/grass' (بر = on, گیا = plants/grass/straw), so the whole clause reports that Kandāvari was seated on the grass inside the mill.
segments.s770-g145: Structure: verb-centered clause with an indirect speech introducer. بدو is the dative/prepositional element 'to him' placed before the verb; گفت is the reporting verb 'said' (literary past). مهتر is the subject 'the elder/chief'. کز introduces a relative/conditional clause 'who/that', ایدر = 'here', and بپوی is a literary present/imperative-form of پوییدن 'seek/pursue' (here meaning 'whoever seeks from here'). The whole segment yields 'the elder said to him (that) whoever seeks from here...'.
segments.s770-g146: چنین 'thus/this way' is an adverb modifying the reported speech; هم 'also/too' reinforces 'also say'; به is the preposition 'to'; ماهوی سوری appears to be a proper name or epithet in apposition (here the addressee to whom the message should be given); بگوی is the imperative/optative form of گفتن 'say/tell' (literary). The clause instructs: 'tell this as well to Mahavi Suri.'
segments.s770-g147: نباید is an impersonal modal 'must not/should not' that sets up a prohibition; که is the complementizer 'that'; آن بدنژاد پلید is a noun phrase 'that base-born, vile (one)' functioning as the subject of the subordinate clause. This segment establishes who is forbidden from the action named in the following clause.
segments.s770-g148: چو here is the literary conjunction 'when'; این 'this' refers back to the preceding matter; بشنود is the literary subjunctive/present of شنیدن 'he hears' (used as a temporal clause 'when he hears this'); گوهر آرد پدید is a verbal phrase: گوهر 'gem' (object) + آرد (present/past stem of آوردن 'brings') + پدید (literary infinitive/adverbial 'to light/into being'), literally 'bring a gem to light'. Together they complete the prohibition: 'must not, when he hears this, bring forth a gem.'
segments.s770-g149: سبک here functions as an adjectival/adverbial qualifier 'light/careless' (literary: light‑minded or lightly); مهتر is the subject 'the chief/elder'; او را marks 'him' as the direct object; بمردی is an archaic adverbial phrase related to 'man/manliness' (here marking the manner or recipient, i.e. 'to a man' / 'manfully' in literary usage); سپرد is the past 'entrusted/handed over' (سپردن). The clause means the chief lightly/without concern handed him over to someone.
segments.s770-g150: جهان‌دیده is an epithet/noun 'world‑seen' (i.e. experienced, worldly-wise) functioning as the next direct object; را is the definite object marker; پیش means 'before/in the presence of'; ماهوی is the proper name 'Mahavi' (addressee); برد is the literary past of بردن 'took/led/brought'. The clause says that the experienced/worldly one was brought before Mahavi.
segments.s770-g151: بپرسید is the past tense 'asked' (literary); ماهوی is the subject (Mahavi, here a proper name); زین is an archaic/formal particle (often 'from/this' or linking word) that connects the question to the person addressed; چاره‌جوی is the object 'seeker of remedy' (the person being questioned). Together: 'Mahavi asked this seeker of a cure...'.
segments.s770-g152: This is an indirect question introduced by که. برسم is a literary subjunctive/optative of رسیدن 'that I may reach/attain' (here 'that I reach' used in the sense 'whom I should go to/reach'); کرا is an archaic interrogative 'who/whom'; خواستی is the past second‑person 'you wished/wanted'; راست گوی is a fixed collocation 'speak truly' (راست 'true' + گوی, here an imperative/verb 'say' in literary usage). The full clause asks the seeker to 'tell truly whom you wanted me to reach.'
segments.s770-g137: This is a prepositional/nominal phrase describing place and appearance. به بالا = 'upward/above' (preposition + adverb); the second به introduces a comparative/manner phrase 'به کردار' = 'in the manner/form of'. کردار here denotes 'bearing/appearance' rather than a literal 'deed'. سرو سهی is a poetic apposition 'cypress (سرو) + سهی (dark/poetic epithet)' giving the image 'dark cypress-like.' Together the phrase modifies the (omitted) subject's position and look.
segments.s770-g138: بدیدار functions as a sight/meeting noun phrase ('to/for the sight' or 'before the view'); it governs خورشید ('the sun') so the sense is 'in the sight of / before the sun.' با + فرهی is an instrument/description 'with noble bearing' (با = 'with', فرهی = 'noble/cultured') that further characterizes how he appeared before the sun.
segments.s770-g139: دو ابرو کمان is structured as 'two brows, (like) a bow' — the noun (دو ابرو) is followed by an appositive/comparative (کمان) giving the shape. و links to the next phrase: دو نرگس دژم = 'two narcissus‑eyes, sullen' where نرگس is a poetic epithet for beautiful eyes and دژم is a postpositive adjective 'sullen/grim' describing them.
segments.s770-g140: This is a pair of verbless descriptions stacked together. دهن پر ز باد = 'a mouth full of (the) wind/breath' where پر = 'full (of)' and ز is the poetic 'from/of' linking to باد ('breath/wind'). ابروان پر زخم = 'the eyebrows full of wounds' with the same construction: plural noun (ابروان) + پر زخم ('full of wounds'). Each unit describes facial features with compact postpositive modifiers.
segments.s770-g141: ببرسم is a first‑person form meaning 'I may/should reach' (conditional/subjunctive sense here). همی is an emphatic particle ('indeed/just'). واژ is an adjectival/participial notion 'overturned'; خواهد گرفت is a future construction (خواهد + گرفت) meaning 'it will take/seize' — together giving a result clause: if I reach, it will indeed be overturned/taken.
segments.s770-g142: سزدگر operates as a concessive/optative marker 'it is fitting/appropriate'; بمانی is second‑person subjunctive/optative 'you remain' or 'that you remain'; ازو = 'of/from it' (i.e., about it); در شگفت = 'in wonder/astonishment.' The clause means 'it would be fitting that you remain amazed by it.'
segments.s770-g143: یکی 'a certain/one' introduces the object; کهنه modifies چبین as 'old Chubīn' (چبین here treated as a proper name or a named object). نهادم is the first‑person past 'I placed/put'; به پیش is the prepositional phrase 'before/in front of (someone).' The whole says 'I placed a certain old Chubīn before (him/you).'
segments.s770-g144: برو is the imperative 'go' (an order or dismissal). نان کشکین = 'meagre/lean bread' (نان = bread; کشکین = thin/lean). سزاوار خویش is a possessive/relational phrase 'fitting to one's own/one's lot' — خویش is the reflexive possessive 'one's own' (here 'that befits you/him'). Together it commands the addressee to go and take/eat the poor bread appropriate to them.
segments.s770-g25: از ایدر = 'from here' (از + adverbial ایدر). تو را is the emphatic/dative object construction ('for you' / 'to you') used with ننگ باشد to express 'it is a shame for you'. ننگ باشد شدن is a poetic periphrasis: باشد (copula/modal) + شدن (infinitive/verb) yields 'to become / to be (considered) a shame'. Word order is somewhat inverted for emphasis (تو را before the predicate).
segments.s770-g26: به یاری = 'by/with the help (of)'; here به carries the instrumental sense 'by/with'. ماهوی qualifies یاری as 'spiritual/essential' (an adjectival nuance). و links the two infinitival actions; بازآمدن is an infinitive/activity noun 'to return'. Together they express 'to be aided by a spiritual (power) and to return.'
segments.s770-g27: به + name marks the indirect object/recipient: 'to Bersam'. فرمای is an imperative/honorific form meaning 'command / tell'. تا introduces a purpose or result clause ('so that' / 'in order that'). با سپاه is a prepositional phrase 'with an army'.
segments.s770-g28: بیاری شود is a poetic compression equivalent to 'with help be made/sent' (بیاری = بِ + یاری, 'with help'; شود is the verbal copula/subjunctive 'let it be / be made'). سوی آن رزمگاه is the directional phrase 'toward that battlefield'. The clause expresses an order that someone be sent toward the battlefield with aid/companions.
segments.s770-g29: به گفتار + صفت شدن: به گفتار سوری شوی means 'you become (or would be made) by the speech of Suri/ceremonial speech' — به + noun + شدن expresses becoming characterized by that noun ('become by/through speech'). سوی جنگ is the directional complement 'toward battle'. سوری here qualifies گفتار ('festive/ceremonial' or 'stirring' speech).
segments.s770-g30: سبکسار precedes the verb as the name/epithet applied to the addressee ('light‑headed' / 'frivolous'). خواند is a past tense verb here meaning 'called' or 'named' (he called you). ت + را together form ترا 'you' (object): ت is the 2nd‑person clitic and را is the definite/object marker. مرد سنگ is the subject placed at the end ('the man of stone' / 'stone-hearted man').
segments.s770-g31: چنین گفت بیژن is a common word order (thus said Bijan) with گفت used as the reporting verb (here introducing direct speech). که introduces reported content ('that'). اینست رای is a nominal clause 'this is (the) counsel/plan' (اینست = 'this is'; رای = 'opinion/plan').
segments.s770-g32: مرا خود نجنبید باید ز جای expresses necessity with an archaic word order: مرا (emphatic/experiencer pronoun 'me' used here with intransitive sense), خود 'myself', نجنبید is a negative jussive/imperative 'do not move', and باید is the modal 'it is necessary / I must'. ز جای = 'from the place'. Together: 'As for me, myself, it is necessary that I do not move from my place.'
